purpose: Provides historical base rates for how long general-purpose technologies take to produce aggregate productivity gains. Answers: when will AI produce measurable TFP improvement?
components: Electricity: ~30 years from invention to aggregate productivity breakthrough
IT/computing: ~15 years from widespread adoption to productivity acceleration
Multiplicative transmission chain: micro gains × adoption rate × reallocation efficiency × complementary investment
Each multiplicative term currently well below 1.0 for AI
current_assessment: AI firm-level gains are real (14% customer support, 30-40% writing tasks) but aggregate nonfarm TFP remains at ~1.5-2.0% with no structural break. Base rates suggest 5-15 years for material aggregate impact. The contrarian argument (AI is recursive and deploys over existing digital infrastructure) self-assessed at 4/10 confidence.
limitations: Historical analogues may not apply to a recursive technology deploying over existing infrastructure
U.S.-China technology decoupling could extend diffusion beyond base rates
Sample size is inherently small (only a few GPTs in history)
Neither the acceleration nor deceleration argument is currently falsifiable
